title: Adverbs people misuse
why_grouped_en: These -ly adverbs sound advanced, so learners reach for them — and use them wrong. Each has a precise job. Learn what they really mean, and when NOT to use them.
why_grouped_mm: ဒီ -ly adverb တွေကို မှားသုံးတတ်ကြတယ်။ တကယ့်အဓိပ္ပာယ်နဲ့ ဘယ်အချိန်သုံးရမလဲ လေ့လာပါ။

words.short_en: in a real, exact way — not exaggerating
words.explanation_en: Use it when something is actually, exactly true — word for word, with no exaggeration.
words.nuance_en: In careful English, don't use it just for emphasis ("I literally died laughing"). That's the opposite of its real meaning.

examples.en: He translated the text literally, word for word.
The town's name literally means "new bridge".

words.explanation_en: Signals the truth, often against what was expected or said. Great for polite corrections.
words.nuance_en: Not the same as "currently" or "now". "Actually, it's free" = contrary to what you thought.

examples.en: It looks hard, but it's actually quite easy.

words.short_en: in the main; to put it simply
words.short_mm: အခြေခံအားဖြင့်
words.explanation_en: Introduces a simple summary of something more complex. "Basically, we ran out of time."
words.nuance_en: Useful, but a filler if overused. Drop it if the sentence works without it.

examples.en: Basically, the plan is to leave early and avoid traffic.
It's basically the same as the old one.

words.explanation_en: Flags that you're being frank, often before an honest opinion. Can also show mild frustration.
words.nuance_en: "Honestly, I didn't enjoy it" = candid opinion. "Honestly!" alone = annoyance.

examples.en: Honestly, I'm not sure this will work.
I honestly didn't know about the meeting.

words.short_en: in a serious way; really (for emphasis)
words.short_mm: အပြင်းအထန်
words.explanation_en: Two jobs: (1) in a serious manner — "take it seriously"; (2) spoken emphasis — "seriously good".
words.nuance_en: "Seriously?" on its own asks "really?" in surprise or doubt.

examples.en: You should take this deadline seriously.

exercises.prompt: Exact, word-for-word meaning: "The phrase ___ translates as 'good morning'."
Polite correction: "___, the meeting is on Friday, not Thursday."
Simple summary of something complex: "___, we just need more time."
Before a frank opinion: "___, I think we should start over."
Treat with importance: "They don't ___ take customer feedback ___." (take ... seriously)
